// SLIDE 01 — HOOK

CHAPTER 3 — READING A FIGMA FILE PROGRAMMATICALLY

A Figma file is a tree. Every visible object on the canvas is a node. Every invisible container — frames, groups, pages

Chapter 3 — Reading a Figma File Program — The tree has a consistent shape at the top:
NARRATION

A Figma file is a tree. Every visible object on the canvas is a node. Every invisible container , frames, groups, pages , is a node. The document itself is a node: the root. The tree has a consistent shape at the top:

// SLIDE 02 — WHERE VARIABLES LIVE

WHERE VARIABLES LIVE.

Variables are not embedded in the node tree. This surprises people. They appear at the top level of the file response, a

Where Variables Live —
NARRATION

Variables are not embedded in the node tree. This surprises people. They appear at the top level of the file response, alongside the document, in their own section.

// SLIDE 03 — COMPONENTS AND STYLES: TWO PLA

COMPONENTS AND STYLES: TWO PLACES EACH

Components appear in two places in the response, for two different purposes.

Components and Styles: Two Places Each — At the top level, components is a flat index — a map from component ID to metadata. Each entry has the component's name,
NARRATION

Components appear in two places in the response, for two different purposes. At the top level, components is a flat index , a map from component ID to metadata. Each entry has the component's name, description, stable library key, and the ID of its component set if it belongs to a variant group. This is what you use for a component inventory: you iterate the map, you do not need to touch the document tree at all.

// SLIDE 04 — WHAT IS NOT IN THE RESPONSE

WHAT IS NOT IN THE RESPONSE.

Document node tree — Yes — document key
Components flat index — Yes — components key
Styles flat index — Yes — styles key
Variables and collections — Only on Enterprise plan — variables key
NARRATION

Prototype interactions are absent. The connections between frames, the trigger types, the animation settings, the smart-animate configurations , none of this is in the document graph returned by GET /v1/files/:key. It exists in the Figma editor, but it does not come through the REST surface this chapter works with. Font files are absent. You can see which font families and weights are in use by inspecting the style properties on TEXT nodes. You cannot download the fonts through the Figma API.

// SLIDE 05 — FIGMA-READ.MJS

FIGMA-READ.MJS

const retryAfter = parseInt(res.headers.get('Retry-After') — '10', 10);
console.error(`Figma API error${res.status} — ${body.err — 'unknown'}`);
const componentMap = data.components — {};
descriptionmeta.description — '',
NARRATION

The tool that makes this chapter concrete is figma-read.mjs. It fetches the file, writes a local fixture, and produces three outputs: a component inventory, a variable inventory, and a missing-description report. It is the second named CLI artifact in this book. The .mjs extension signals ES module format. Node 18 and later support top-level await in ES modules , use it here rather than wrapping everything in an immediately-invoked async function.

// SLIDE 06 — THE WALK FUNCTION

THE WALK FUNCTION.

The walk function is three lines long and is the foundation of every traversal in this book:

The walk Function —
NARRATION

The walk function is three lines long and is the foundation of every traversal in this book:

// SLIDE 07 — THE LOCAL FIXTURE

THE LOCAL FIXTURE.

The fixture written by figma-read.mjs is not a nice-to-have. It is a core practice.

The Local Fixture — A full file fetch on a large design system takes several seconds and consumes rate-limit quota. Figma imposes rate limit
NARRATION

The fixture written by figma-read.mjs is not a nice-to-have. It is a core practice. A full file fetch on a large design system takes several seconds and consumes rate-limit quota. Figma imposes rate limits on the REST API . If you are iterating on extraction logic , debugging a variable resolver, adjusting a traversal, testing a new output format , hitting the live API on every run means slow feedback loops and the real possibility of exhausting your quota mid-session.

// SLIDE 08 — THREE READ STRATEGIES

THREE READ STRATEGIES.

GET /v1/files/key — Full audits, complete traversals, any analysis where you do not know in advance which nodes you need
GET /v1/files/key?depth=N — Structural reads: page names, top-level frame types, checking node presence without traversing subtrees
GET /v1/files/key/nodes?ids=ID1,ID2,... — Targeted extraction: you have a list of component IDs from a prior structural pass and need
NARRATION

Not every question about a Figma file requires the full 47,000-line response. There are three read strategies, each suited to different questions. GET /v1/files/:key , the full fetch , returns the complete document tree, all component metadata, all style metadata, and the variables block. Use this when you need to traverse the full graph: complete audits, compliance checks, any analysis where you do not know in advance which nodes you will care about. Accept that this is slow on large files. Write the fixture after the first fetch.

// SLIDE 09 — THE OUTPUT SHAPE

THE OUTPUT SHAPE.

figma-read.mjs produces three files. These are the stable interface for everything downstream.

The Output Shape — component-inventory.json is a flat list of all component definitions: names, IDs, descriptions, and a boolean hasDescrip
NARRATION

figma-read.mjs produces three files. These are the stable interface for everything downstream. component-inventory.json is a flat list of all component definitions: names, IDs, descriptions, and a boolean hasDescription. It is consumed by documentation sync in Chapter 10, audit tooling in Chapter 5, and specification generation in Chapter 12.

// SLIDE 10 — FAILURE MODES

FAILURE MODES

The large-file timeout.→The partial variable response.→Stale node IDs.→The archive page problem.→Instance vs. definition confusion.
NARRATION

Understanding how this script fails is as important as understanding how it works. The large-file timeout. For files with hundreds of components and deep nesting, the full fetch can take a long time , sometimes tens of seconds. If the connection drops before the response completes, you get an error rather than a partial response. There is no partially-delivered JSON here; the API returns the full body or nothing.

// SLIDE 11 — WHAT COMES NEXT

WHAT COMES NEXT.

You can now read any Figma file programmatically. You have a fixture for offline development. You have a component inven

What Comes Next — The gap that remains is not about reading. It is about trust. You can extract what the file contains; you cannot yet det
NARRATION

You can now read any Figma file programmatically. You have a fixture for offline development. You have a component inventory, a variable inventory, and a missing-description report , three files that are the stable input for every downstream tool in this book. The gap that remains is not about reading. It is about trust. You can extract what the file contains; you cannot yet determine whether the file is structured in a way that a pipeline can rely on.

// SLIDE 12 — THESIS

THE CORE CLAIM.

You can now read any Figma file programmatically. You have a fixture for offline development. You have a component inven

The gap that remains is not about reading. It is about trust. You can extract what the file contains; you cannot yet det

NARRATION

You can now read any Figma file programmatically. You have a fixture for offline development. You have a component inventory, a variable inventory, and a missing-description report , three files that are the stable input for every downstream tool in this book. The gap that remains is not about reading. It is about trust. You can extract what the file contains; you cannot yet determine whether the file is structured in a way that a pipeline can rely on.

// SLIDE 13 — CLOSE

ASK THE QUESTION. APPLY THE FRAMEWORK.
